preFix.title: Shadow puppet papercutting
preFix.subtitle: Cutting a black card silhouette for shadow play
preFix.excerpt: Shadow puppets are cut from black card stock as clean silhouettes, designed in profile for maximum readability against a lit screen. This tutorial covers silhouette design, cutting sequence from interior to outer edge, and attaching a bamboo skewer handle.
preFix.sourceNotes: The shadow puppet tradition documented in Southeast Asian Wayang Kulit sources (public domain by age) and in Théodore Pavie, Etudes sur l'Inde (1848, public domain). The paper-cutting craft in this tutorial draws on general silhouette-cutting principles common to the European cut-silhouette tradition documented in 19th-century craft sources, and on the Wayang Kulit decorative perforation technique. The specific paper-cutting steps are synthesised from general paper-cutting method.

content.text: Shadow puppets are among the oldest forms of cut-paper art. The Wayang Kulit tradition of Southeast Asia produces extraordinarily detailed figures from water-buffalo hide; the simpler paper version cuts from black card and works just as well for shadow play against a backlit sheet. The key design rule is that the figure is shown in profile: a side view reads as a clear recognisable shape when projected.
This tutorial covers cutting a single-piece profile figure and attaching a bamboo skewer handle. An optional jointed arm section is described at the end.

items.qty: One or two sheets, depending on figure size
items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm, black
items.substitutions: White 220 gsm cartridge painted on both sides with black acrylic works well. The card must be stiff enough to hold its shape without a handle but thin enough to cut cleanly with a craft knife.

items.qty: 1 or 2 (30 cm length for a hand-held puppet)
items.name: Bamboo skewer

items.qty: Small amount, for attaching the skewer handle
items.name: PVA, regular
Tracing paper
items.substitutions: Optional; for transferring a drawn template onto the black card.

items.name: Brass paper fastener
items.substitutions: Optional; for a single jointed limb. Not needed for a single-piece puppet.

attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt blades; blunt blades skid. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing cutting mat. Always cut against a metal ruler for straight cuts, with the non-cutting hand at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path, never in front of it. Cut away from the body.

content.text: Designing the silhouette
Draw the figure in profile on plain paper first, not on the black card. A
profile silhouette
shows the figure from the side: head, body, arms, and legs all visible in one connected outline. Avoid full-face views; a face seen straight-on loses most of its features in silhouette.

Keep the outline bold and clear. Thin protruding shapes; fingers spread wide, long hair strands, narrow waists; are fragile and easy to tear during cutting. Simplify them: hair as a single solid mass, hands as a mitten shape or a held object. The best shadow-puppet silhouettes read from three metres away.
Mark the handle area as a solid block at the base of the figure (below the feet or at the centre of the torso) with no cuts in it. This is where the skewer attaches.
Transferring the design

Lay a sheet of tracing paper over the drawn design and trace the outline in pencil. Flip the tracing paper over so the pencil marks face downward, then tape it lightly onto the black card. Rub firmly over the back of the tracing with the flat side of the pencil. The graphite on the face-down side transfers to the black card as a faint but legible line.
The transfer line on black card is hard to see. Hold the card at a raking angle to a lamp; the slight graphite sheen catches the light and makes the line visible.
Cutting sequence: interior first
Begin with any
negative space cuts

inside the silhouette. These are openings cut within the body of the figure that do not touch the outer edge. Common examples: the space between an arm and the torso, gaps in clothing, eye holes. The Wayang Kulit tradition makes very elaborate openings here; simple star or diamond perforations read clearly against a lit screen.

Use the bookbinding awl to pierce decorative perforations: hold the card on the cutting mat and push the awl through with a single firm downward press. For small geometric openings (circles, diamonds), a series of closely spaced awl holes creates the pattern without needing to cut the shape.

Cut the outer silhouette last. Work slowly around the shape with the craft knife, keeping the blade vertical. On curves, rotate the card rather than the knife; small rotation steps rather than one sweeping arc. Cut all the way around in a single session; do not leave partly-cut sections and return to them later.
Attaching the handle

Lay the cut figure face-down. Apply a small amount of PVA to the first 4 cm of the bamboo skewer. Press the skewer against the handle area of the figure, centred across the body, with the skewer pointing downward from the figure's feet. Hold it in place for a minute, then leave flat to dry for ten minutes.

A second skewer attached at a shallow angle to the lower body gives a more secure grip for active manipulation. Bind the two skewers together with a length of thread wrapped tightly several times around the join, then sealed with a further drop of PVA.
Finishing

Let the PVA cure fully for at least thirty minutes before testing the puppet against a light source. Hold the puppet between a lamp and a white sheet or translucent screen: the image appears as a clear dark silhouette. The closer the puppet is to the screen, the sharper the shadow; the further away, the softer and larger the silhouette.
Care

Store finished puppets flat between sheets of paper in a folder. The card is stable indefinitely in dry storage. The skewer handle may detach if the PVA joint is stressed before it has fully cured; leave overnight before vigorous use.
Variations

A single jointed arm gives the figure one moving part. Cut the arm as a separate piece with a small solid tab at the shoulder end. Align the tab over the corresponding position on the main body, mark the pivot point, and punch a hole through both pieces with the bookbinding awl. Push a
brass paper fastener
through both holes from the front and spread the prongs on the back. The arm now pivots at the shoulder. A second skewer glued to the hand end of the arm provides independent control.
